name: Cognitive Load Theory + Hick's Law + Gestalt Principles
definition_origin: Three convergent frameworks: (1) Cognitive Load Theory — John Sweller (UNSW, late 1980s) defining intrinsic / extraneous / germane load on working memory. (2) Hick's Law (Hick-Hyman Law) — William E. Hick (1952) and Ray Hyman (1953) on logarithmic relationship between number of choices and decision time: RT = a + b·log2(n+1). (3) Gestalt Principles — Berlin School of psychology, Wertheimer, Köhler, Koffka (1910s-1930s): proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, figure/ground, common fate, common region. All three are foundational pre-attentive / cognitive constraints exploited in interface minimalism.
core_mechanism: Working memory holds ~4 ± 1 chunks (Cowan 2001) — anything beyond this leaks. Cognitive Load Theory says interfaces should minimize extraneous load (visual noise, redundant labels, gratuitous decoration) while preserving intrinsic load (the actual task). Hick's Law says decision time grows logarithmically with choices — categorization beats long flat lists because users mentally binary-search through grouped options. Gestalt principles operate pre-attentively (sub-100ms) — humans perceive grouped/aligned/repeated elements as wholes before conscious processing, so layout that respects Gestalt is parsed faster and feels 'cleaner'. Together they predict that minimalist, well-grouped, choice-pruned interfaces feel effortless and produce higher satisfaction — the cognitive cost is invisible because it never occurs.
neuroscience_correlate: Hick-Hyman has been mapped to the cognitive control network (dorsolateral PFC, anterior cingulate cortex) by Wu et al. (2018, PMC5998988) — RT scaling correlates with ACC engagement on choice difficulty. Gestalt grouping has been linked to V2/V4 visual cortex activity (Kourtzi & Kanwisher) and the lateral occipital complex. Working memory capacity in CLT is linked to dorsolateral PFC and parietal cortex activity (Baddeley's working-memory model maps to prefrontal-parietal circuits).
empirical_evidence: (1) Hick's Law: Hick (1952) original n=2 subjects, replicated thousands of times in HCI; Proctor & Schneider (2018) review confirmed log relationship holds across 60+ years of choice-RT experiments with caveats on practice and stimulus-response compatibility. (2) Cognitive Load: Sweller's CLT meta-validated across decades; instrument validation (Frontiers in Psychology 2017) confirms intrinsic/extraneous/germane separability. (3) Gestalt: NN/g 'Proximity Principle' confirmed via eye-tracking — grouped elements scanned in fewer fixations. (4) Industry validation: Material 3 Expressive (2025, 18,000 participants, 46 studies) found expressive yet well-grouped UIs achieve up to 4x faster element recognition vs. baseline — operationalizing all three frameworks. (5) Google search homepage and iOS Settings consolidation are cited as canonical minimalism-driven satisfaction wins. (6) SUS / NASA-TLX scales operationalize subjective load.
measurable_outcome_metric: Time-to-task-completion, error rate, NASA-TLX cognitive-load score, eye-tracking fixation count and saccade length, recognition speed (M3 Expressive's metric), SUS score, drop-off rate per choice point, decision-time delta after grouping/categorization changes.
engineering_tactics: (1) Cap visible choices: top-level navigation ≤5 items (Hick's Law); use progressive disclosure (Apple HIG) for advanced settings. (2) Group with proximity (8/16/24/32 spacing scale, never random gaps) so pre-attentive grouping does the work. (3) Establish similarity: a single button shape, two type weights, three colors max as default state — variation reserved for emphasis. (4) Closure: skeleton screens trigger closure perception, making perceived load lower. (5) Reduce extraneous load: remove redundant labels, kill decorative dividers, prefer whitespace over rules. (6) Preserve germane load: chunk multi-step flows (≤7 steps), show progress indicators, save-as-you-go to reduce memory burden. (7) Mobile: pin frequent actions in thumb zone (75% of users are thumb-only); use bottom-sheets to show contextual choices instead of long lists. (8) Web: keyboard-first menus + Cmd+K command palettes (Linear, Raycast, Vercel) consolidate hundreds of choices into a single search-rankable surface — the optimal Hick's Law collapse.
real_world_examples: (1) Google Search homepage — single text input is the canonical Hick's Law collapse; decades-stable because every additional choice cost satisfaction. (2) iOS Settings (iOS 17+) — categorized into ~12 top groups with progressive disclosure, where iOS 6 had a flat 30+ list. (3) Linear command bar — collapses 200+ actions into a single Cmd+K surface; choices are ranked dynamically so the perceived choice count is ≤5. (4) Apple Calculator — strict Gestalt grid; same color signals same function (digits white, operators orange). (5) Notion's slash menu — Hick's Law via just-in-time inline command palette, replacing a toolbar with 30+ icons. (6) Things 3 task list — extreme proximity discipline; same baseline grid for every row reduces fixations. (7) Stripe Checkout — extraneous load eliminated (no logo, no nav), three fields visible, completion rate cited as conversion-best in class.
mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile is more cognitive-load-sensitive: smaller viewport amplifies any clutter and thumb travel adds physical cost to extra choices. Apply Hick's Law more aggressively (≤4 tab-bar items per Apple HIG, max 5 per Material). Web has more screen real estate for parallel choices but suffers more from decision paralysis on long lists; command palettes (Cmd+K) are the web's killer mitigation. Gestalt principles apply on both; mobile rewards stronger proximity grouping because there is no hover state to disambiguate weakly grouped clusters.
platform_design_language_alignment: Strongly aligned with both. Apple HIG explicitly cites 'aesthetic integrity' and progressive disclosure; Liquid Glass (iOS 26+) tries to add visceral richness without violating Gestalt grouping. Material 3 Expressive empirically validates that minimalist + expressive can coexist — recognition speed up to 4x faster — provided grouping discipline is intact. Ship-faster guidance: do not fight either platform; respect their grid systems and component density.
claude_design_application: Instruct Claude to apply explicit numeric caps and grouping. Example prompt fragment 1: 'Build a settings screen. Hard caps: ≤5 items per group, ≤4 groups visible without scroll. Use 16dp gap within group, 32dp between groups (proximity). Single button shape across the screen (similarity). Use only weight 400 and 600 of Manrope (no italics, no all-caps). For any group with >5 items, collapse into a Show all CTA with progressive disclosure.' Example prompt fragment 2: 'Audit the screen for cognitive load: list every visible interactive element, justify its presence (intrinsic load), or flag for removal (extraneous load). Output the audit as a comment block above the component before writing code.'
stitch_application: Stitch needs explicit grouping and quantity rules because it cannot infer Gestalt. Example prompt fragment 1: 'Mobile dashboard. Three Gestalt groups separated by 32dp vertical gap. Group A (top): 4 stat cards in a 2x2 grid, 16dp gap, identical shape (12px corner radius), only the icon and accent color differ. Group B (middle): single hero CTA card, full width, 56dp tall. Group C (bottom): list of 5 most-recent items, 16dp row height, divider lines OFF (use whitespace not rules).' Example prompt fragment 2: 'Tab bar must show exactly 4 items. Use system icons + label, identical icon size 24dp, identical label weight. No badges except on the Notifications tab. Forbid more than 4 tabs.'
ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI defaults to dumping every imaginable feature/choice on screen — long flat nav, 6+ tabs, decorative dividers between every row, redundant labels, mixed corner-radii, mixed icon styles. This blows up extraneous load and Hick's Law. Counter-prompt: 'Hard-cap visible top-level choices at ≤5. No decorative dividers — use whitespace at the 8/16/32dp scale instead. One corner-radius value across the screen. One icon library (Phosphor, Lucide, or SF Symbols — pick one and stick to it; do not mix). Output the choice count and grouping audit before writing the JSX.'
anti_patterns: (1) Mega-menus on mobile (defeats Hick's Law). (2) Decorative dividers between every list row (extraneous load + breaks proximity grouping). (3) Mixed corner-radii (e.g. 8px on cards, 16px on buttons) — destroys similarity grouping. (4) 'Surface every feature' nav with 8+ tabs. (5) Tooltips that hide critical info (cognitive cost of recall > recognition). (6) Modals stacked on modals (working-memory overflow). (7) 'Customize every setting' onboarding screens that present 20 toggles upfront — extraneous load at the worst moment.
regulatory_risk: Low. Reducing cognitive load is generally pro-user. Minor risk: aggressive simplification can hide consent or pricing information — DSA / GDPR require informed consent visibility, so do NOT use cognitive-load arguments to bury legal disclosures or auto-renewal terms (this becomes a dark pattern). California ADPPA, EU DSA, and FTC Click-to-Cancel impose visibility requirements on cancellation/consent paths regardless of 'simplicity' claims.
pitfalls_caveats: (1) Over-minimalism — hiding too much breaks discoverability (the 2010s 'hamburger everywhere' era). (2) Hick's Law has known caveats: practice, stimulus-response compatibility, and category structure all moderate it (Proctor & Schneider 2018) — log scaling is not absolute. (3) Cognitive Load's intrinsic/germane separation is contested in education research (some authors collapse them). (4) Accessibility: extremely sparse interfaces can be harder for screen-reader and cognitive-impairment users — provide explicit landmarks and labels. (5) Performance: pre-attentive Gestalt grouping requires consistent rendering — sub-pixel jitter or layout shift breaks the perception.
cross_cultural_validity: Largely universal: Gestalt principles and Hick's Law are biologically grounded (visual cortex, motor planning) and replicate across cultures. Cognitive Load Theory replicates broadly in education across East Asian and Western samples. Cultural calibration applies to information density tolerance — Japanese, Chinese, and Korean users tolerate higher density (e.g. Yahoo Japan, Naver, Tmall) without subjective overload, while Western users prefer more whitespace. RTL languages require mirrored grouping but the underlying principles hold.
sources: [Hick-Hyman Law mediated by cognitive control network — PMC 2018](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5998988/)
[Element Interactivity and Intrinsic, Extraneous, Germane Load — Springer](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10648-010-9128-5)
[Proximity Principle in Visual Design — Nielsen Norman Group](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/gestalt-proximity/)
